header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: Full Simulation -- Danielle Leads the Room (Final Review)
lesson.menu_desc: The final review and the debut it prepares you for: your first high-stakes meeting with the Canadian Maple Bear leadership. Three tasks, no script -- open the meeting and frame your strategic mandate, present your People & Culture plan for the Canadian division, and field two unscripted questions under pressure. This lesson reactivates the whole program: the executive toolkit (mandate, stakeholder, buy-in, to move the needle, to read the room, developmental feedback, to empower), and every structure you built -- future perfect and continuous, past perfect, used to / be used to, advanced modals, wish / if only, third conditional, cleft sentences and hedging -- all in authentic production. A celebration and a test: this is where you sound like the same senior leader in English that you already are in Portuguese. -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 15 -- Final Review & Simulation
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 15 -- Final Review & Simulation

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Grant Whitfield, VP of the Canadian division. I am leaving this the week before you fly in, because I wanted you to hear the shape of the meeting from me and not from a calendar invite. On your first Thursday, you are opening the leadership session. It will be me, the academic lead, and two of our regional directors, and I will be honest with you, everyone in that room has read your Brazil numbers and they are impressed, and everyone in that room is also quietly wondering whether the person who did all that in Portuguese can do it here, in English, in front of us. That is the real test, and pretending otherwise would not help you. So here is what I am going to ask of you. Open the meeting yourself. Frame your mandate, tell us what you were actually brought here to do, and do not undersell it. Then walk us through your People and Culture plan for the Canadian division, ten minutes, no slides you hide behind, just you and the thinking. And at the end I am going to ask you two questions you will not have prepared for, because that is where I find out who somebody really is. One of them will probably be uncomfortable. I want you to know that is by design, and it is not hostility, it is interest. The team you built in Brazil did not follow you because your grammar was perfect. They followed you because you saw them clearly and you told them the truth. Bring that person into the room on Thursday. The English is just the vehicle. See you soon.

Danielle, it is Ruth, your coach, and this is the last note I am going to leave you before Thursday, so I want to use it carefully. You have spent fifteen weeks building something, and I need you to take stock of it before you walk in, because you are the kind of person who will remember every word you got wrong and none of the ground you covered. So let me hold up the mirror for a second. Fifteen lessons ago you told me your fear was walking into that room and sounding junior when, in Portuguese, you are anything but. Listen to yourself now. You frame a mandate without apologising for it. You disagree with a doctor and hold your ground without losing the room. You give a struggling person developmental feedback that grows them instead of shrinking them. You read an understatement across a cultural gap that used to be invisible to you. That is not someone hoping to keep up. That is a leader. Here is the only instruction I have for Thursday, and it is not about tenses. When Grant asks you the uncomfortable question, do not reach for the perfect sentence. Reach for the true one. Hedge where you are genuinely unsure, that is honesty, not weakness, and then commit to the part you do know. The most senior thing in any language is not fluency. It is a person who can say I do not have a complete answer yet, and here is how I would find one. You already do that in Portuguese. On Thursday you are just going to do it in English. Go and lead the room. I will be listening for the story afterwards.

items.situation: "Grant opens: 'Before we start, tell the room in one minute what you were actually brought here to do.' Frame your mandate -- do not undersell it."
tips: I was brought in to lead the cultural transformation of the Canadian division -- to design the employee journey, close the trust gaps our diagnostic surfaced, and build the People and Culture function the way I built it in Brazil. By the end of my first year I will have run a full cultural diagnostic, rolled out the new onboarding experience, and given every leader here a clear people strategy to work from.
own the mandate with future perfect ('I will have run / rolled out'); no hedging on what you were hired to do -- save hedging for uncertainty, not for your own remit

items.situation: "A regional director asks: 'How is this different from what your predecessor tried and dropped?' Answer without trashing the predecessor."
tips: My predecessor laid real groundwork, and I would rather build on it than start over. What I am changing is the sequence: he rolled out the framework before he had the buy-in, so it never gained momentum. I am running the listening tour first, earning credibility, and only then moving the needle -- same destination, a different order of operations.
'would rather build on it than' (advanced modal, no blame); past simple for what happened, present continuous for your plan; land 'buy-in / move the needle / gain momentum'

items.situation: "The academic lead, a doctor, pushes: 'With respect, a Brazil-designed framework may not transfer to a Canadian academic culture.' Disagree and hold your ground."
tips: That is a fair challenge, and I would push back gently on one part of it. I am not importing a Brazilian framework; I am importing a diagnostic method and letting your context write the answers. I take the point about cultural distance -- it is exactly why I would rather read this culture first than prescribe to it. Where we may differ is on transfer: the method is culturally neutral, even if the findings will not be.
concede the fair part ('I take the point'), then 'where we may differ' (cleft/hedged disagreement); stand your ground without a flat 'no'

items.situation: "Grant's uncomfortable question: 'What does success look like for you, personally, in this role -- not for the company?' No prepared answer exists."
tips: Honestly, let me think on that out loud. I think success, for me, is the day a Canadian leader here disagrees with me directly, in a meeting, because the psychological safety is real enough that they can. If I have built a culture where people push back on me the way I am pushing back on you now, that is the whole job. I am not fully sure how I would measure it yet -- but that is the thing I would want to be true.
hedge honestly ('let me think on that out loud', 'I am not fully sure yet'), then commit to the true part; a real, personal answer beats a polished corporate one

items.situation: "A director you have not won over yet says your plan is 'ambitious'. Read the understatement and respond."
tips: I am reading 'ambitious' as a gentle way of saying you are not yet convinced it is realistic -- tell me if I have that wrong. If I have it right, that is exactly the conversation I want. I would rather you told me plainly where the plan is thin, because the fastest way to move the needle is for me to hear the real objection now, not after we have launched.
read the room -- 'ambitious' is an understatement/polite objection; surface it with 'tell me if I have that wrong', invite directness without imposing

items.situation: "Close the meeting. In two or three sentences, tell the leadership team what you want them to remember about you after today."
tips: Remember that I came to listen before I came to change anything, and that I told you the truth even when a more comfortable version was available. The English is newer for me than the work is -- but the work is the same work I have done for a decade, and I am going to do it here with the same clarity. Give me a year, and this division will feel like a place people are proud to belong to.
close on identity, not tasks; a touch of vulnerability about the language paired with total confidence about the work -- that contrast is the senior note
